narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. Your profile includes a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the typical student's level, along with middle-family income, full-time student status, a Year 1 school decile of 6—slightly above average—and a Year 3 decile of 2, which is notably lower than most. Your LSAT score of 21.30 stands out as significantly below the average of 36.70 seen in the dataset of past bar exam takers. The model, trained on thousands of similar students' data, predicted a 65% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why the university rejected your application—they use this to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as it was much lower than what typically leads to bar success. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it matched average strong performers. Your lower Year 3 decile ranking then nudged things back toward failure, more so than your slightly above-average Year 1 decile, which helped a bit. Factors like your middle-income background and full-time status provided minor positive influences toward passing. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your LSAT was the biggest gap, while your GPA aligned well, but the dip in later-year performance raised concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted prep. I recommend focusing on boosting your LSAT with intensive practice or retakes, strengthening your later academic showings, and perhaps seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a stronger reapplication next cycle. You've got resilience on your side; use this feedback to pivot and come back even stronger.
